statusLine: EU fuel reserves remain under pressure in March 2026, with 15 of 27 member states holding at least one fuel category below the 90-day mandatory minimum, while jet fuel emerges as the most widespread vulnerability across the bloc.
fullAnalysis:
As of March 2026, the EU's aggregate oil stock position sits at 95.6 days for petrol, 88.1 days for diesel, and 87.0 days for jet fuel. While the petrol average nominally clears the 90-day mandatory minimum, diesel and jet fuel fall short at the bloc-wide level — a meaningful signal given that these fuels underpin freight logistics and aviation respectively. The overall status has been assessed at 'watch', reflecting a system under strain rather than one in acute crisis, but the distribution of stress across member states tells a more sobering story.

At the country level, 5 of 27 member states — Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal — hold critical status across all three fuel categories simultaneously, meaning every measured fuel type falls below mandatory thresholds. Several larger economies also present serious vulnerabilities: France holds a critical petrol position at 69.3 days and critical jet fuel at 65.0 days; Italy is critical across all three categories; and Germany, despite watch-level petrol and diesel stocks, registers a critical jet fuel figure of just 59.9 days. Jet fuel is the single most pervasive weak point: 18 of 27 member states carry critical jet fuel reserves, including major aviation hubs such as Germany, France, the Netherlands, and Denmark. This pattern is consistent with strategic reserve releases reported in response to recent Middle East supply disruptions, which appear to have drawn down aviation fuel stocks disproportionately.

On the price side, the EU petrol average stands at €1.771/L and diesel at €1.781/L as of mid-June 2026. Prices vary substantially across the bloc, with Danish motorists paying €2.392/L for petrol compared to €1.340/L in Malta — a spread driven primarily by differing national tax structures rather than supply costs alone. Brent crude is currently trading at $79.44/barrel, up 9.6% since late February 2026 when the current supply disruption cycle began. This sustained crude price elevation, if it persists or intensifies, will place additional upward pressure on refined product costs and may complicate efforts by member states to replenish drawn-down reserves at acceptable cost.

Several member states demonstrate the system's uneven resilience. Estonia, Ireland, Latvia, and Slovenia maintain comfortable buffers across most or all categories, while countries including Finland and the Netherlands show highly asymmetric profiles — strong in some fuels, critically low in others. The concentration of jet fuel shortfalls across economically significant member states warrants close monitoring as the summer aviation season peaks. Policymakers should note that current EU-wide averages mask the depth of individual country exposures, and any further supply shock could rapidly convert watch-level aggregate figures into more acute stress at the national level.
keyPoints: Diesel and jet fuel fall below the 90-day EU mandatory minimum at the aggregate level (88.1 days and 87.0 days respectively), while petrol narrowly clears it at 95.6 days.

Jet fuel is the most widespread vulnerability: 18 of 27 member states hold critical jet fuel reserves, including major aviation economies Germany (59.9d), France (65.0d), the Netherlands (63.2d), and Denmark (75.7d).
5 of 27 member states — Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal — are critical across all three fuel categories simultaneously, with several recording jet fuel stocks below 25 days.
Brent crude has risen 9.6% since late February 2026 ($72.48 to $79.44/barrel), increasing the cost of reserve replenishment at a time when multiple countries have already drawn down strategic stocks.

Retail petrol prices range from €1.340/L in Malta to €2.392/L in Denmark, reflecting divergent national tax regimes; the EU average of €1.771/L for petrol and €1.781/L for diesel indicates diesel parity or a slight premium in many markets.
